summary: Overall UK risk remains Elevated. The dominant near-term stressor remains external: elevated energy prices linked to Middle East instability and wider geopolitical spillovers, with Brent still around $110/bbl and up materially over the past month. Domestically, there are persistent but manageable pressures: active flood alerts/warnings in parts of England (including one current flood warning), and planned NHS disruption from resident doctors’ industrial action later this week.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Energy-price sensitivity remains the key UK macro risk, with Brent around $110/bbl and up ~11% over the past month; inflation remains above target on the latest official data.
drivers: Brent crude around 109.8 USD/bbl on 6 April, up ~10.9% over the past month
ONS: CPI 3.0% y/y and CPIH 3.2% y/y (February 2026), indicating inflation remains above target
FTSE 100 around 10,436 and GBP/USD around 1.321; no clear market dislocation signal in the cited reference sources

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: The wider security environment remains stressed by the Russia–Ukraine war and Middle East conflict dynamics that can affect energy markets and maritime routes; UK terrorism threat level remains at a raised baseline.
drivers: MI5 national terrorism threat level remains SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely)
Continued Russia–Ukraine conflict activity reported by independent analysts
Ongoing Middle East instability continues to pose spillover risk to global energy and shipping

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: UK governance remains stable, but crisis-management posture remains active given the Middle East situation and cost-of-living sensitivities; recent COBR readout emphasised economic mitigation measures and anti-profiteering enforcement planning.
drivers: GOV.UK COBR(M) readout (23 March) focused on domestic economic impacts of the Middle East situation
Government signalled potential new time-limited CMA/regulatory powers to counter price gouging/anti-profiteering
Employment Rights Act implementation timeline includes measures taking effect from 6 April, indicating ongoing active legislative programme

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: No current Met Office UK weather warnings are listed at the time of update, but flood risk remains present with one active flood warning and multiple flood alerts.
drivers: Met Office UK warnings page shows no current active UK weather warnings
Environment Agency service shows 1 flood warning and 16 flood alerts (flooding expected/possible in affected areas)
Groundwater and coastal flood alerts remain active in multiple locations (localised disruption risk)

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: The main near-term disruption risk remains the confirmed multi-day resident doctors’ strike in England; broader cost-of-living sensitivity persists alongside transport-strike risk signalling in London later in April.
drivers: BMA: resident doctors’ strike in England scheduled 7am 7 April to 6.59am 13 April
NHS systems remain vulnerable to surge/pressure episodes (recent critical-incident declarations in 2026 indicate low slack)
London Underground strike dates are currently advertised for later April and May (potential localised disruption)

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: Cyber risk to UK organisations remains elevated with continuing emphasis on rapid remediation for exploited vulnerabilities and ongoing ransomware activity; NCSC continues to highlight active exploitation affecting widely used enterprise software.
drivers: NCSC: active exploitation reported for SharePoint Server vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-53770 / CVE-2025-53771) and UK organisations urged to apply updates
Security reporting continues to highlight high ransomware activity levels and multi-sector targeting
Operational cyber hygiene tightening continues (e.g., Cyber Essentials scheme updates announced for April 2026)

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics quoted Brent around 109.79 USD/bbl on 6 April, up ~0.7% day-on-day and ~10.9% over the past month.

indicators.name: GBP/USD
indicators.note: Wise mid-market converter displayed ~1 GBP = 1.321 USD (no timestamp shown on page).

indicators.name: FTSE 100 (UKX)
indicators.note: TradingView showed UKX around 10,436.30 with a +0.69% daily move on the page snapshot.

indicators.name: UK inflation (CPI/CPIH)
indicators.note: ONS inflation and price indices page shows CPI and CPIH annual rates unchanged versus January for February 2026.

indicators.name: Flood status (England)
indicators.note: Environment Agency flood service lists one flood warning and sixteen flood alerts (counts at time of reading).

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat level
indicators.note: MI5 indicates the national threat level from all forms of terrorism is SUBSTANTIAL.

changes_since_last_update: Natural Hazards reduced (Elevated → Guarded) because the Met Office UK warnings page shows no current active weather warnings at time of update, although flood alerts/warnings remain active.
Market indicator values updated (Brent, GBP/USD, FTSE 100) using latest reference quotes; overall status and other category levels held steady.
priority_watch_items: Energy price shock risk: monitor Brent and any escalation affecting oil/gas supply routes (Hormuz/Red Sea) and UK pump prices.
Flooding: monitor whether Environment Agency flood warnings/alerts expand or escalate, especially groundwater/coastal areas.
NHS disruption (7–13 April): monitor operational impacts of resident doctors’ strike and any local critical-incident declarations.
Exploited vulnerabilities: ensure rapid patching for widely used enterprise systems and monitor NCSC advisories.
Public order/transport disruption: monitor confirmation of any London Underground/rail strike action later in April and associated service impacts.
source_notes: Market indicators taken from reference quote pages (Trading Economics for Brent; Wise for GBP/USD; TradingView for FTSE 100) and may lag intraday moves.
Inflation data taken from ONS inflation and price indices landing page (February 2026 release).
Flood counts from the Environment Agency ‘Check for flooding’ service; Met Office warnings based on the Met Office UK warnings page snapshot.
Strike timing taken from BMA press release; transport-strike information uses public advisory pages and may change.
Cyber posture notes reflect NCSC guidance on actively exploited vulnerabilities and public reporting on ransomware activity; incident attribution and sector impacts can be incomplete or lagging.
